headline: CRUDE RUNS 3%; BEANS AND WHEAT FIRM BACK
subheadline: WTI posts its biggest single-session gain in weeks as Hormuz diplomacy reshapes the energy story; grains catch a modest bid; cattle slip.
lead: WTI crude ran $2.40 to $77.67 Monday, the biggest one-session gain in several weeks, as Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, flipped into a different kind of story: Iran's oil exports through Hormuz hit a wartime high after the U.S. lifted the naval blockade, and Kuwait is tendering Gulf loadings for the first time in months. That's not a fear bid in crude. That's a supply-flow normalization bid, and it has different legs. Soybeans firmed 6 cents to $11.48 and wheat added 2 cents to $6.16, both catching modest reflex bids after last week's break, but with USDA Crop Progress dropping at 3 PM CT today, neither move carries conviction yet.
the_takeaway: Crude's move today is supply normalization, not fear; those have different staying power.
teaser: Crude ran hard on Hormuz normalization. Beans and wheat caught a small bounce. Cattle slipped. Crop Progress at 3 PM tells the grain story.

one_number.unit: barrels per day, India's Russian crude imports in June
one_number.context: India is on pace for a record high volume of Russian crude imports in June, according to OilPrice.com, as Hormuz-linked disruption pushed the world's third-largest importer hard into Moscow's barrels. This matters for US ag because Indian demand for US crude alternatives softens as Russian supply flows, and it tells you the Hormuz premium unwind has real trade-flow consequences, not just futures pricing. Watch whether the India-Russia relationship persists after Hormuz normalizes; structural demand shifts take longer to reverse than sentiment.

yesterdays_call.summary: Cattle on Feed data confirmed the bull thesis; live cattle at $254.80 earned the move on June 19.
yesterdays_call.note: Live cattle settled $246.75 today, well off the $254.80 print; the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout and processing constraints kept pressure on, and the bull thesis from the CoF data hasn't held.

sections.title: CRUDE LEADS THE COMPLEX
sections.body: WTI crude ran to $77.67, up 3.2%, the session's clearest story and the only overnight surprise worth naming today. The catalyst is specific: Iran's oil exports through Hormuz hit a wartime high after the U.S. lifted the naval blockade outside the chokepoint, and Kuwait tendered Gulf naphtha loadings for the first time in months as regional producers race to move barrels. **This is not a geopolitical fear bid.** Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, are reshaping into a supply-normalization trade: more oil moving, not less, and the price response reflects improved flow expectations rather than disruption risk. Natural gas added 1.8% to $3.27, consistent with the broader energy firmness. For producers watching diesel and fertilizer input costs, the crude move is a one-session read; the structural Permian gas build, up 60% since 2021 per EIA, is the longer-run input cost story.
sections.catalyst: Iran oil exports through Hormuz hit wartime high; U.S. lifted naval blockade; Kuwait resumes Gulf loadings.
sections.bottom_line: Crude ran on supply normalization, not fear; those moves have different staying power.

sections.title: GRAINS CATCH A BREATH
sections.body: Soybeans added 6 cents to $11.48 and wheat firmed 2 cents to $6.16, both modest reflex bids after last week's sustained break. **Corn was essentially unchanged at $4.44**, off a quarter cent, and that tells you the grain complex doesn't have a new driver yet. It's waiting on one. That driver arrives today at 3 PM CT when USDA releases Crop Progress. The standing situation coming into this report: corn planting reached 42% as of the week ending May 24, and the Belt has been running ahead of the 5-year average despite a wet May start. The question for today's print is how much further planting and crop ratings have advanced through the wet June pattern. Seedling disease pressure from excessive Midwest and Delta rains, flagged by BASF's Mike Probst, is showing up in soybean fields specifically, with Rhizoctonia and Pythium concerns where seed treatments are only partially effective. If the Crop Progress report shows soybean condition ratings softening, the 6-cent bounce in beans today could have more to build on. If ratings hold steady, the bounce is thin.
sections.catalyst: USDA Crop Progress due 3 PM CT; soybean seedling disease pressure reported in Midwest and Delta from excessive rain.
sections.bottom_line: 3 PM CT decides whether today's grain bounce has a story behind it or not.
sections.farmer_action: Hold old-crop sales until after the 3 PM Crop Progress print; a weak condition rating adds premium the morning session hasn't priced.

sections.title: CATTLE SLIP, PROCESSING MATH UNCHANGED
sections.body: Live cattle eased to $246.75, off about 0.9%, and feeders held nearly flat at $366.92, down a tenth of a percent. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began Tuesday May 19 is still the frame: 1,700 workers locked out, roughly 6,000 head daily removed from processing capacity, and no resolution announced. **The split between feeders and live cattle that defined last week's action has partially broke down** as live cattle gave back more ground. A few cash cattle deals cleared Nebraska at $256 live last week, about a dollar above the prior weighted average, which is nominally supportive, but the futures board isn't trading that premium today. A beef magazine analysis released this morning pushes back on the narrative that packer concentration is driving consumer beef prices, arguing demand is the primary driver. That's a longer structural argument; the short-run processing constraint from the lockout is what's moving the calendar spread.
sections.catalyst: Cargill lockout processing constraint ongoing; Nebraska cash cattle $256, steady-to-firm but not moving futures.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle acting like a buyer who's present but not urgent; lockout resolution is the line that resets this.

sections.title: MACRO HOLDS, DOLLAR FLAT
sections.body: The S&P 500 added 1.1% to 7,500.58 and the US Dollar Index slipped a tenth of a percent to $100.85, both minor moves that do nothing to complicate the grain or livestock story today. **Gold dipped a tenth to $4,163, silver added 1%.** The macro backdrop is not the lead this week; the grain calendar is. USDA also released a 2027 cost-of-production forecast showing farm production costs on track for record highs, with no meaningful relief projected. That's not a trading catalyst for today's session, but it is the context in which producers are making fall-crop decisions. Feeding margins have improved in recent weeks as corn, soybean meal, and wheat futures pulled back, per Beef Magazine, but the 2027 cost-of-production data says the structural cost pressure hasn't broken.
sections.catalyst: USDA 2027 cost-of-production forecast projects record-high farm costs; S&P +1.1% on no specific ag catalyst.
sections.bottom_line: Record-high input cost forecast is the budget story behind today's quiet futures.

outside_the_pit.title: Sharp gain at Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG Injures Dozens, 18 Missing
outside_the_pit.body: A fire and sharp gain at Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex Sunday injured more than 50 people and left 18 unaccounted for as of Monday morning. Qatar is calling back empty LNG carriers to prepare for export restart despite ongoing repairs. This is the kind of supply-chain event that takes weeks to quantify, and it adds an LNG-specific risk layer on top of the broader Hormuz normalization story.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm Pressure Continues on Both Sides of the Border
outside_the_pit.body: Texas and Mexican ranchers are dealing with New World screwworm in a situation that has no clean resolution date. The pest's spread has direct implications for US cattle imports from Mexico and for summer pasture management decisions across the southern Plains. Ranchers operating near the border should be watching USDA APHIS updates closely.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Projects Farm Production Costs Hitting Record Highs in 2027
outside_the_pit.body: USDA's newly released cost-of-production forecast sees total farm costs continuing to rise into 2027 with no meaningful relief projected. Against the backdrop of the North Dakota State study estimating active China tariffs cost US ag $15 billion annually, the cost side of the farm income equation is getting squeezed from both ends. Lock in input contracts where the calendar allows.

spread_to_watch.label: Soybean Oil / Soybean Meal share of crush value
spread_to_watch.level: Soybean oil at $66.59, up 1.0%; soybean meal at $304.10, up 0.3%; oil share moving wider.
spread_to_watch.commentary: Soybean oil outpaced meal by a factor of 3 to 1 today, and that ratio has been quietly shifting all month as biofuel demand props the oil side while meal sits under export pressure. When oil share runs ahead of meal share, it tells you the crush is being driven by renewable diesel demand, not protein demand, which changes who's buying the beans and why. If the oil share keeps widening, the crush margin story for fall beans looks different than the protein-export story that dominated spring.

basis.headline: Corn basis soft in the west, beans firming selectively in the east.
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis is holding firm as ethanol grind stays active, but the western Belt is running soft with ample old-crop supply still working through the system. **Soybean basis is showing selective firming east of the Mississippi** ahead of the Crop Progress report; merchandisers near crush facilities are bidding for beans where seedling disease concern is creating origin uncertainty. Old-crop producers with stored beans in the eastern Belt have a window the board alone isn't fully pricing. Watch whether that firming holds through Tuesday once the Crop Progress data is digested.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress show soybean condition deterioration from Midwest seedling disease, adding weather premium to the board?
weekly_thread.status_text: The 3 PM CT Crop Progress report is Monday's make-or-break data point: if soybean ratings soften from wet-weather disease pressure, today's 6-cent bounce in beans has a foundation. If ratings hold, the bounce is a head fake and the prior week's break reasserts. This is the week's question because pollination is approaching and the condition trajectory set this week will price the next leg.

the_more_you_know.title: What soybean oil's 1% gain today is really pricing.
the_more_you_know.body: Soybean oil added 1.0% to $66.59 while soybean meal only added 0.3%, a gap that looks small but carries a structural message. Crush facilities produce both products in a fixed ratio, and when the market values one side significantly more than the other, it signals which demand center is driving the crush. Right now, renewable diesel mandates and biofuel blenders are bidding for soybean oil at levels that support crush margins even when meal export demand is soft. That's why the soybean complex can hold up even as protein demand from China's $17 billion annual US ag purchase commitment through 2028 has not yet translated into sustained futures support. The oil-side bid is the floor the market is standing on, not the export ledger. Producers pricing new-crop beans should understand they're selling into a crush margin supported by biofuel policy, and any change to RFS blending mandates hits the oil-side bid first.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: soybean good-to-excellent rating below last week's level adds disease-weather premium; corn planting above 85% removes any residual planting-pace concern.
Weekly Export Sales: soybean sales under 300,000 MT puts the chart back in charge; above 500,000 MT gives the bounce a fundamental floor.
Cargill Fort Morgan lockout resolution: any announcement resets the live cattle technical picture from $246.75 upward; no resolution keeps pressure on.
Agriculture meteorologist El Nino update livestream (Beef Magazine): Intermountain West drought and monsoon moisture forecast matters for summer pasture conditions across the western range.
Ras Laffan LNG restart progress: facility damage assessment and carrier return timeline will reprice natural gas basis in export-linked markets if the restart is delayed beyond two weeks.

daily_quote.text: Wealth consists not in having great possessions, but in having few wants.

source_summary: USDA NASS Cattle on Feed; USDA Crop Progress (due 3 PM CT); OilPrice.com Iran/Kuwait Hormuz export reporting; Brownfield Ag News soybean disease report (BASF); Farm Policy News USDA 2027 cost-of-production forecast; Beef Magazine cash cattle and packer concentration; CME Group locked price table.
